summary: This PR adds zstd (Zstandard) decoding support to httpx via a new `ZStandardDecoder` class and a `zstd` optional dependency. The implementation has two critical correctness issues: the zstd sentinel uses `False` instead of `None` (inconsistent with brotli), and the `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` dict is never cleaned up when zstd is unavailable, causing runtime `ImportError` instead of graceful fallback. Test coverage for the new decoder is also significantly lacking across error paths, streaming, and multi-frame scenarios.

findings.title: zstd sentinel uses `False` instead of `None`, and `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` is never cleaned up when zstd is unavailable
findings.detail: When zstandard is not importable or is too old, `zstd` is set to `False` (a bool) instead of `None` — inconsistent with the `brotli = None` pattern used throughout the same file. More critically, there is no code to remove `'zstd'` from `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` when the package is absent (compare the `if brotli is None: SUPPORTED_DECODERS.pop('br')` block in `_decoders.py`). As a result, if a server returns `Content-Encoding: zstd` and zstandard is not installed, the decoder is found in the dict, `ZStandardDecoder.__init__` is called, the `if zstd is False` guard raises `ImportError`, and the request fails at runtime instead of gracefully falling back. Fix: use `None` as the sentinel in `_compat.py`, update the `if zstd is False` guard in `_decoders.py` to `if zstd is None`, and add a `if zstd is None: SUPPORTED_DECODERS.pop('zstd')` block analogous to the brotli one.

findings.title: ZStandardDecoder error paths (flush incomplete-stream, decode error) are untested
findings.detail: `ZStandardDecoder.flush()` raises `DecodingError('Zstandard data is incomplete')` when `self.decompressor.eof` is False, but this branch is never exercised. The parametrized `test_decoding_errors` test in `tests/test_decoders.py` covers `deflate`, `gzip`, and `br` but not `zstd`. A truncated or invalid zstd payload that triggers the error path in both `decode()` and `flush()` should be added to the test suite.

findings.title: Multi-frame zstd decoding loop is untested and has no infinite-loop guard
findings.detail: The while-loop (`while self.decompressor.eof and self.decompressor.unused_data`) handles concatenated zstd frames, but: (1) it is completely untested — the only test uses a single-frame payload and never exercises this branch; (2) if a buggy or adversarial zstandard library leaves `eof=True` and `unused_data` unchanged after decompression, the loop will spin forever. A defensive fix is to break if the new `unused_data` equals the old one. A test with a multi-frame zstd body should also be added.

findings.title: `test_empty_content` and `test_decoders_empty_cases` do not cover `zstd`
findings.detail: Both parametrized tests cover `deflate`, `gzip`, `br`, and `identity` but not `zstd`. The behaviour of `ZStandardDecoder` when given an empty byte string is untested and should be added to both parametrize lists.

findings.title: Streaming (chunked) zstd decoding is untested
findings.detail: The existing `test_zstd` feeds the entire compressed body as a single call to `decode()`. There is no streaming test analogous to `test_streaming` (which covers gzip with an async iterator), so partial-frame delivery across multiple `decode()` calls is completely untested.

findings.title: Regex match result not checked for None before calling .groups()
findings.detail: The call `re.search(r"^([0-9]+)\.([0-9]+)", zstd.__version__).groups()` will raise `AttributeError` at runtime if `zstd.__version__` does not match the pattern (e.g. an empty string, a purely alphabetic dev tag, etc.). The `# type: ignore[union-attr]` comment suppresses mypy's warning but provides no runtime safety. This `AttributeError` is not caught by the outer `except (AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError)` because it occurs in the `else` branch after a successful import. Fix: assign the result to a variable, check for `None`, and fall back to `zstd = False` (or `None`) if unmatched, e.g.: `m = re.search(...); if m is None: zstd = False`.

findings.title: Version-check fallback path and the resulting ImportError in ZStandardDecoder are untested
findings.detail: The branch that sets `zstd = False` when the installed version is older than 0.18 is marked `# Defensive:` with no corresponding test. Similarly, the `ImportError` raised in `ZStandardDecoder.__init__` is marked `# pragma: no cover`. There should be at least one test (using mocking or monkeypatching) that verifies this fallback: i.e., when an old zstandard is present, constructing `ZStandardDecoder` raises `ImportError`.

findings.title: `import re` added solely for a one-off version parse that could use str.split()
findings.detail: The file previously only imported `ssl` and `sys`. Adding `import re` to parse `zstd.__version__` is heavier than necessary; a simple `zstd.__version__.split('.')` would accomplish the same version comparison without the new import.

findings.title: Incomplete/placeholder ImportError message in ZStandardDecoder.__init__
findings.detail: The `ImportError` message string `"Using 'ZStandardDecoder', ..."` contains literal `...` placeholder text. It should be replaced with a complete, descriptive message analogous to the `BrotliDecoder` error message pattern used elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Inconsistent indentation for new `zstd` optional-dependency entry
findings.detail: All other entries in `[project.optional-dependencies]` (`brotli`, `cli`, `http2`, `socks`) use 4-space indentation for the dependency value. The new `zstd` entry uses 2-space indentation, deviating from the surrounding style.

findings.title: Section heading says 'brotli and zstandard' but code block only shows one pip command at a time
findings.detail: The heading was changed to 'To include the optional brotli and zstandard decoder support' but the code block still shows `pip install httpx[brotli]` and `pip install httpx[zstd]` as separate commands without individual headings. The heading implies a combined instruction, which is misleading. Consider giving each command its own heading/paragraph matching the original style, or adjusting the heading to match the two-step presentation.

findings.title: Broad exception tuple `(AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError)` is undocumented
findings.detail: The brotli block only catches `ImportError`. The wider `except (AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError)` tuple here catches additional exception types without a comment explaining which failure scenario each covers. At minimum, inline comments should document why `AttributeError` and `ValueError` are expected.

findings.title: Line broken mid-sentence inconsistently with surrounding prose style
findings.detail: The replacement text breaks the sentence after `will`, leaving `not be automatically decoded.` on a new line. The surrounding prose paragraphs are written as single unbroken lines; this mid-sentence break is inconsistent with that style.

findings.title: zstd set to False (bool) instead of None when unavailable, breaking truthiness checks
findings.detail: When zstandard is not importable or is too old, `zstd` is set to `False` (a boolean). In `_decoders.py` the guard is `if zstd is False`, which works for that specific check, but in `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` the removal logic only removes `br` when `brotli is None` — there is no corresponding removal of `zstd` when it equals `False`. That means `ZStandardDecoder` remains in `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` even when `zstd` is unavailable, and any response with `Content-Encoding: zstd` will attempt to construct a `ZStandardDecoder`, enter `__init__`, hit the `if zstd is False` guard, and raise an `ImportError` at runtime instead of simply not supporting the encoding. The brotli pattern uses `None` consistently; this PR mixes `False` vs `None` in a way that breaks the SUPPORTED_DECODERS cleanup path.

findings.title: Missing removal of 'zstd' from SUPPORTED_DECODERS when zstandard is not installed
findings.detail: The brotli decoder is removed from `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` when `brotli is None` (line 365-366). There is no analogous code to remove `'zstd'` from `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` when `zstd is False` (i.e., when the zstandard package is absent or too old). As a result, if a server returns `Content-Encoding: zstd` and the package is not installed, the decoder will be looked up successfully in the dict, `ZStandardDecoder.__init__` will be called, and an `ImportError` is raised instead of falling back gracefully. A block like `if zstd is False: SUPPORTED_DECODERS.pop('zstd')` must be added.

findings.title: Multi-frame zstd decoding loop does not guard against infinite loop on zero-length unused_data
findings.detail: The while-loop condition is `while self.decompressor.eof and self.decompressor.unused_data`. If somehow `unused_data` is non-empty but `decompress(unused_data)` leaves `eof=True` and `unused_data` unchanged (e.g., due to a bug or edge case in the zstandard library), the loop would spin forever. A more defensive approach would be to break if the new `unused_data` equals the previous one, or to limit iterations. This is a low-probability edge case but worth guarding against.

findings.title: Version regex match result not checked for None before calling .groups()
findings.detail: The call `re.search(r"^([0-9]+)\.([0-9]+)", zstd.__version__).groups()` will raise `AttributeError` if `zstd.__version__` does not match the regex (e.g., a dev or non-standard version string like `'0.18.0.dev0'` would actually match, but something like an empty string or unexpected format would return `None`). The `# type: ignore[union-attr]` comment acknowledges this, but at runtime a `None` dereference will produce an `AttributeError` that is not caught by the outer `except (AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError)`, because the `re.search` call is in the `else` branch after a successful import. This should use `match = re.search(...); if match is None: zstd = False` pattern.

findings.title: Unchecked regex match result before calling .groups()
findings.detail: If `zstd.__version__` is an unexpected string that doesn't match the pattern `^([0-9]+)\.([0-9]+)`, `re.search()` returns `None` and calling `.groups()` on it raises `AttributeError`. The `type: ignore[union-attr]` suppresses mypy's warning about this, but doesn't guard against runtime failure. If a malformed/custom zstandard build is installed, this could crash the import. Consider using `re.match` with a fallback, or wrapping this in a try/except, or assigning the match to a variable and checking for None first.

findings.title: Using `False` (a bool) as a sentinel for a missing module
findings.detail: Setting `zstd = False` when the import fails means the type of `zstd` switches between a module and a bool. This can cause subtle issues if any code does `if zstd:` vs `if zstd is False:` inconsistently. A more conventional pattern is `zstd = None`, which is the pattern already used for `brotli` elsewhere in this file. This inconsistency could theoretically cause incorrect gating in `_decoders.py` (`if zstd is False`) vs other potential callers that may check `if zstd:`.

findings.title: Sentinel value `False` used instead of `None` for optional module
findings.detail: The brotli fallback (line 19) sets `brotli = None` on import failure, but the zstd block sets `zstd = False` both on import failure and on version check. This inconsistency makes downstream `if zstd is False` checks diverge from the `if brotli is None` pattern used in the rest of the file. Using `None` throughout would be consistent with the established convention.

findings.title: Incomplete ImportError message – placeholder text left in
findings.detail: The string `"Using 'ZStandardDecoder', ..."` appears to contain placeholder/stub text (`...`). The existing `BrotliDecoder` error message (if any) or similar patterns in the file would be a better reference. The message should describe what the class does and why the import is needed, without leaving `...` as filler.

findings.title: `if zstd is False` guard is inconsistent with how `brotli` is checked
findings.detail: Because `brotli` is set to `None` on failure (not `False`), the brotli decoder guard would read `if brotli is None`. Using `False` here for `zstd` departs from that pattern. If the sentinel is harmonised to `None` in `_compat.py`, this check should become `if zstd is None`.

findings.title: `import re` added for a single version-check that could use `packaging` or simple string split
findings.detail: The existing file imports only `ssl` and `sys`. Adding `import re` purely to parse the version string is heavier than needed; `zstd.__version__.split('.')` would work and avoids the new stdlib import. The surrounding code does not use `re` anywhere.

findings.title: Broad exception tuple `(AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError)` is undocumented
findings.detail: The brotli block only catches `ImportError`. The wider tuple here catches `AttributeError` and `ValueError` without a comment explaining which specific failure scenario each covers. The `# Defensive:` label alone is not enough; the brotli block sets a precedent of plain `ImportError` only.

findings.title: Inconsistent indentation in `[project.optional-dependencies]` section
findings.detail: All other optional-dependency entries (`brotli`, `cli`, `http2`, `socks`) use 4-space indentation for the dependency value. The new `zstd` entry uses 2-space indentation, which deviates from the surrounding style.

findings.title: Line broken mid-sentence in a way inconsistent with surrounding prose
findings.detail: The original sentence was a single flowing line. The replacement breaks after `will`, leaving `not be automatically decoded.` dangling on a new line. The surrounding untouched prose paragraphs are written as single unbroken lines; the line break here is inconsistent with that style.

findings.title: Section heading says 'brotli and zstandard' but code block only shows `httpx[brotli]`
findings.detail: The heading was changed to 'To include the optional brotli and zstandard decoder support' but the shell block underneath still shows only `pip install httpx[brotli]` followed separately by `pip install httpx[zstd]`. This makes the heading feel misleading; it would read more clearly if the heading stayed singular (per the original style) or if the two commands were given separate headings/paragraphs each matching the existing pattern.

findings.title: Multi-frame zstd stream decoding path has no test
findings.detail: The while loop at line 163 (`while self.decompressor.eof and self.decompressor.unused_data`) handles the case where a response body contains multiple concatenated zstd frames (each frame is decompressed, unused_data is fed into a new decompressor, and so on). The only test in `test_zstd` uses a single-frame payload and never exercises this branch. There is no test with a multi-frame zstd body.

findings.title: ZStandardDecoder.flush() incomplete-stream error path has no test
findings.detail: The `flush()` method raises `DecodingError('Zstandard data is incomplete')` when `self.decompressor.eof` is False after flushing. This branch is never exercised by the existing tests. `test_decoding_errors` in `tests/test_decoders.py` covers deflate, gzip, and br but not zstd, so a truncated/invalid zstd payload that triggers this path is untested.

findings.title: `test_decoding_errors` parametrize does not include 'zstd'
findings.detail: The parametrized test `test_decoding_errors` covers `deflate`, `gzip`, and `br` but not `zstd`. An invalid/truncated zstd payload should raise `httpx.DecodingError`; that behaviour (exercised through `ZStandardDecoder.decode` and `ZStandardDecoder.flush`) is not tested.

findings.title: `test_empty_content` and `test_decoders_empty_cases` do not include 'zstd'
findings.detail: Both parametrized tests (`test_empty_content` and `test_decoders_empty_cases`) cover `deflate`, `gzip`, `br`, and `identity` but not `zstd`. The behaviour of `ZStandardDecoder` when given an empty byte string is untested.

findings.title: Version-check fallback (`zstd = False` for version < 0.18) is marked `# Defensive:` with no test
findings.detail: The branch that sets `zstd = False` when `_zstd_version < (0, 18)` is marked `# Defensive:` (similar to `# pragma: no cover`) but there is no test that installs/mocks an older version of zstandard and verifies that `ZStandardDecoder.__init__` raises `ImportError` in that case. The `ImportError` path in `ZStandardDecoder.__init__` (line 152-156 of `_decoders.py`) is also marked `# pragma: no cover`, so both the compat fallback and the downstream ImportError are entirely untested.

findings.title: Streaming (chunked) zstd decoding not tested
findings.detail: The existing `test_zstd` feeds the entire compressed body at once. There is no streaming test for zstd analogous to `test_streaming` which tests gzip with an async iterator. The `decode()` method receiving partial frames across multiple calls is untested.

findings.title: zstd sentinel uses `False` instead of `None`, and `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` is never cleaned up when zstd is unavailable
findings.detail: When zstandard is not importable or is too old, `zstd` is set to `False` (a bool) instead of `None` — inconsistent with the `brotli = None` pattern used throughout the same file. findings.title: Line broken mid-sentence inconsistently with surrounding prose style
